coredata.dc:title: Carbons confined silver nanoclusters decorated carbon fiber electrodes toward electrochemical sensor of dihydroxyphenol and heavy metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Zhi-Yuan
Jiang, Jin-Chi
Meng, Long-Yue
coredata.dc:description: The development of electrochemical sensors based on noble metals has presented remarkable opportunities and challenges for the detection of trace phenols and heavy metal ions. However, the high costs of precious metals hinder the practical implementation of electrode materials. Enhancing the utilization efficiency of precious metals on the electrode surface is a crucial step toward improving electrochemical sensors based on these valuable materials. In this study, the morphology and utilization rate of silver nanoparticles were regulated by decorating nanofilms with different dimensions on carbon fiber electrodes. Specifically, the polypyrrole-derived carbon film coating enabled the material to have a large electroactive area and silver loading capacity. Simultaneously, the limiting effect of graphene quantum dots formed silver nanoparticles into silver clusters and achieved excellent electrochemical detection performance for dihydroxyphenol and heavy metal ions. Compared with silver nanoparticles, silver clusters provide higher electrocatalytic power and atomic utilization of silver. This study provides technical support for the trace analysis of environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon fibers
Electrochemical sensor
Polypyrrole
Graphene quantum dots
Silver nanoclusters

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical behaviors of the modified electrode
Electrochemical detection of HQ and CC
Electrochemical detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+
Anti-interference, stability, and reproducibility of the electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of CFs(a), PPY/CFs(b), NC/CFs(c), Ag/NCs/CFs(d) and Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs(e). (f) is the scanning element mapping of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs.
XRD patterns of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs (a), Raman spectra of CFs, GDs/CFs, GDs/NCs/CFs (b) and TGA curves of PPY/CFs (c).
XPS spectrum of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs(a) and deconvoluted C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Ag 3d XPS signals of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs(b–e).
CV curves of CFs, NCs/CFs, GDs/NCs/CFs and Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs detected by 0.2 mM HQ, CC in 0.1 M, pH 7.2 PBS buffer solution.

Cyclic voltammetry curves of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs against 0.2 mM HQ, CC(a) at scan rates of 25 mV/s–200 mV/s, potential window −0.2 V–0.6 V. Linear fitting curves of the scan rate square root-oxidative peak current intensity of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs for HQ(b), CC(c), respectively.

Typical amperometric i-t response of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs on HQ(a), CC(b) in the concentration range of 1 μM–800 μM in 0.1 M, pH 7.2 PBS buffer solution; fitting curves of HQ(c), CC(d) peak currents as a function of concentration.
DPV curves of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs for various concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+ (a, b) and corresponding linear relationship between I and the concentration (c, d).
Analytical performance comparison of the Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs for HQ, CC, Cd2+ and Pb2+ detection with other reported electrodes.
Determination of HQ, CC Cd2+ and Pb2+ in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Carbons confined silver nanoclusters decorated carbon fiber electrodes toward electrochemical sensor of dihydroxyphenol and heavy metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yanbian University, Park Road 977, Yanji 133002, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Yanbian University, Park Road 977, Yanji, 133002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Science, Yanbian University, Park Road 977, Yanji 133002, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Science, Yanbian University, Park Road 977, Yanji, 133002, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Yanbian University, Park Road 977, Yanji 133002, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Co-first authors: Zhi-Yuan Feng, Jin-Chi Jiang.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of electrochemical sensors based on noble metals has presented remarkable opportunities and challenges for the detection of trace phenols and heavy metal ions. However, the high costs of precious metals hinder the practical implementation of electrode materials. Enhancing the utilization efficiency of precious metals on the electrode surface is a crucial step toward improving electrochemical sensors based on these valuable materials. In this study, the morphology and utilization rate of silver nanoparticles were regulated by decorating nanofilms with different dimensions on carbon fiber electrodes. Specifically, the polypyrrole-derived carbon film coating enabled the material to have a large electroactive area and silver loading capacity. Simultaneously, the limiting effect of graphene quantum dots formed silver nanoparticles into silver clusters and achieved excellent electrochemical detection performance for dihydroxyphenol and heavy metal ions. Compared with silver nanoparticles, silver clusters provide higher electrocatalytic power and atomic utilization of silver. This study provides technical support for the trace analysis of environmental pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of society has brought progress to human civilization; however, environmental problems have become increasingly prominent. In recent years, phenolic substances and heavy metal ions have been found in water, soil, and food, which poses an urgent problem to be solved [1–3]. Excessive intake of dihydroxyphenol and heavy metal ions can result in fatigue, nausea, visceral edema, and liver and kidney damage, and can affect the entire nervous system [4–7]. Conventional analytical techniques for the detection of phenols and heavy metal ions include fluorescence [8], high-performance liquid chromatography [9], and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [10]. However, these techniques involve multiple steps, extensive sample collection, and high costs. Electrochemical techniques, a collection of techniques that use electrical simulations to analyze the reactivity of a solution, allow the rapid and sensitive detection of pollutants [11,12]. Wang et al. designed a composite material composed of polyaniline (PANI) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) that compensates for the lack of g-C3N4 conductivity through its excellent electron transport capacity, thus achieving the electrochemical detection of Cd2+ [13]. Fan et al. used Longquan lignite to prepare porous carbon for the detection of hydroquinone (HQ) and catechol (CC) and studied the effects of different pore structures on the electrochemical properties [14]. However, in food and environmental samples, coexisting electroactive components result in interference, which hinders electron transfer between the target and the electrode and, consequently, sensitivity, accuracy, and reproducibility. Hence, improving the anti-interference ability and selectivity of the electrode to design an electrode material that can simultaneously analyze and detect numerous substances is crucial.

Carbon fibers (CFs) have recently been used as substrates for electrode materials due to their high electrical conductivity and flexibility [15–17]. However, the chemically inert surface of unmodified CFs hampers the separation of the oxidation peaks of various target phenols, impeding the enrichment of heavy metal ions. Carbon surfaces require functionalization via plasma treatment [18,19], chemical oxidation [20], and heteroatom doping [21]. Among them, heteroatom doping is the most common for the modification of carbon materials, and N doping can effectively improve the surface wettability of carbon materials and enhance electron transfer via hydrogen bonds formed between the lone electron pairs of N and the hydroxyl groups of phenolic substances [22,23]. Polypyrrole (PPy), a nitrogen-rich conducting polymer, is typically used as a nitrogen-doped carbon precursor for in-situ nitrogen doping after carbonization. PPy-based carbon exhibits excellent electron transfer ability and high electrical conductivity. Functional groups such as –NH2 and CN on its surface can adsorb metal ions and promote nanoparticle formation [24–26].

Among multidimensional nanomaterials, zero-dimensional nanomaterials have broad application prospects in the field of electrochemistry owing to their small size and large specific surface area. Graphene quantum dots (GDs), a novel type of zero-dimensional graphene sheets with lateral dimensions of less than 100 nm, display excellent optical and electronic properties and result in confinement effects that can effectively limit the morphology and distribution of metal nanoparticles [27–29]. Compared with other noble metal nanomaterials, silver nanoparticles (Ag-NPs) are increasingly used in various fields owing to their commercial availability and easy preparation process [30,31]. Sudhakara et al. [32] reported the synthesis of a novel electrocatalyst by incorporating tetra-amino cobalt (II) phthalocyanine into conductive polyaniline, which was decorated with AgNPs. The synthesized electrode allowed simultaneous detection of HQ and CC. The sensor exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity. However, further improvement in the dispersion and atomic utilization of Ag-NPs is required. Compared to dispersed noble metal nanoparticles, nanocluster-like structures usually have better electrocatalytic ability, which can greatly improve atom utilization. Therefore, converting Ag-NPs into clusters is an effective method to improve Ag utilization.

In this study, a facile electrodeposition method was developed to prepare one-dimensional PPy-derived N-doped carbon films and zero-dimensional GDs films on the surfaces of carbon fibers to regulate the morphology and dispersion of AgNPs. Under the localized action of GDs, AgNPs changed from a single distribution to a flower cluster structure that was uniformly distributed on the surface of the carbon film. Finally, rapid detection of HQ, CC, Cd2+, and Pb2+ was achieved. Electrodeposition can be used to easily control the thickness and morphology of functional films, which has obvious advantages.

CFs were first calcined in a muffle furnace at 450 °C for 30 min, then refluxed in acetone for 12 h for desizing, and finally subjected to plasma ashing for 30 s. PPy was modified on the CFs surface by electrodeposition, and the pyrrole concentration, deposition time, and deposition voltage were adjusted to obtain the optimal film thickness for PPy/CFs. PPy/CFs were carbonized under N2 atmosphere to form NC/CFs, with a temperature range of 400–500 °C and heating rate of 0.3, 0.5, and 1.0 °C min−1. Using phosphate buffer solution (PBS) as the electrolyte, an aqueous GDs dispersion solution was added, and GDs were deposited on the CF surface by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The potential ranged from −2.0 to 1.2 V, and the scan rate was 50 mV s−1 for 20 laps. Finally, Ag-NPs were electrodeposited using CV in AgNO3 (0.1 M) with KNO3 as the electrolyte, with voltage ranging from −0.2 to 0.6 V, and a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for 5 laps. The obtained samples were denoted as Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs. As a control, NCs/CFs were used to modify Ag-NPs. GDs and Ag-NPs were directly modified on the surface of the CFs and annotated as Ag/CFs and Ag/GDs/CFs, respectively.

The morphological and structural features of the samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM; SU8000, Hitachi, Japan). Elemental compounds were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB220i-XL, VG Scientific, UK). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA-400, PE) was used to study the changes in PPy/CFs under N2 atmosphere, with a temperature range of 25–800 °C and a rate of 5 °C min−1. The samples were analyzed using a microconfocal Raman spectrometer. The lattice structure of the Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs was characterized using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Aeris PANalytical, France).

Electrochemical measurements were performed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI760E, Chenhua Co., Shanghai, China) equipped with a traditional three-electrode system. The prepared composites were used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and platinum wire as the counter electrode. Electrochemical detection of HQ and CC was performed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and chronoamperometry in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.2). The CV potential ranged from −0.2 to 0.6 V. The electrochemical detections of Cd2+ and Pb2+ were performed by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) in sodium acetate buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 3.5). The pulse amplitude was set at 50 mV, the potential increment at 5 mV, the pulse period at 100 ms, and the pulse width at 50 ms.

Flexible CFs with a diameter of approximately 7.4 μm were used as electrode substrates to prepare the nanohybrid carbon fiber electrodes for the detection of HQ, CC, Cd2+, and Pb2 (Fig. 1a). The plasma surface treatment enriched the fiber surface with oxygen-containing functional groups, thereby promoting PPy deposition. During electrodeposition, dense and rough PPy was coated onto the CF surface (Fig. 1b). After carbonization, PPy was converted into carbon. The pyrrole monomer led to a nitrogen-doped carbon film that turned into a core-shell structure and retained the rough surface structure of PPy, effectively increasing the electrode-electrolyte contact surface area and the diffusion and adsorption/desorption rates of the electrode. Fig. 1d and e shows the difference in the morphology of the Ag-NPs before and after GDs-modification, respectively. Ag-NPs were granular and individually and irregularly dispersed on the surface of the PPy-derived nitrogen-doped carbon film. The modified GDs provided sufficient active sites for the nucleation of Ag-NPs and imparted a confinement effect on size control, thus promoting the surface assembly of the Ag-NPs. The Ag-NPs aggregated from several individually nucleated particles to form silver nanoflower clusters anchored to the material surface. Fig. S1 shows the morphological differences between the modified Ag-NPs before and after GDs decoration on the CF surface. The average Ag-NP size was larger, and the GDs decoration caused the Ag-NPs to aggregate into clusters. Energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) mapping further revealed that C, O, N, and Ag were uniformly distributed on the surface of the Ag/GD/NC/CF electrode.

Fig. 2a shows a typical XRD pattern of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs. The diffraction peaks at 38.2°, 44.2°, 64.4°, and 77.4° are attributed to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of the face-centered cubic structure of Ag, respectively [32]. Raman spectroscopy investigated the structures of CFs, GDs/CFs, and GDs/NCs/CFs, with two peaks at 1359 and 1589 cm−1 corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. CFs, GDs/CFs, and GDs/NCs/CFs had I D/I G ratios of 1.015, 0.976, and 0.909, respectively (Fig. 2b). The intensity of the D band is related to the disordered carbon content, whereas the intensity of the G band represents the extent of sp2 hybridization and degree of graphitization [33]. Upon the introduction of PPy, the intensity of the two peaks of the GDs/NCs/CFs increased, whereas the I D/I G ratio decreased indicating a good degree of graphitization, which was beneficial for the overall conductivity enhancement of the material. The thermal stability of the prepared PPy/CFs was evaluated via TGA under N2 atmosphere. The pyrolysis process for PPy/CFs performed at 0–800 °C exhibited three stages (Fig. 2c). During the first stage (0–150 °C), mass loss was mainly attributed to the physically adsorbed water and a small amount of residual solvent in the PPy precursor. During the second stage (150–300 °C), the mass loss was mainly due to the crystallization process and unstable oxygen-containing functional groups in PPy/CFs. In the third stage (300–800 °C), the mass loss was due to the degradation of the PPy chain and unstable carbon in the material affected by the combustion of the carbon skeleton.

XPS analysis provided insights into the elemental composition of the composite materials. The plasma treatment enabled the CFs to obtain abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, whereas the introduction of PPy increased the N content of the composites (Fig. S2). After electrodeposition, a small amount of Ag was deposited. The full XPS spectrum of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs (Fig. 3a) shows the sole contributions of C, N, O, and Ag, indicating the high purity of the composites, which is in accordance with the EDS elemental mapping. The corresponding fitting curves for the four elements are shown in Fig. 3b–e. The Ag spectrum shows binding energies at 374.2 and 368 eV, corresponding to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, respectively, which is consistent with previously reported results [34]. The deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum consisted of four peaks at 284.9, 285.7, 287.2, and 288.5 eV attributed to the C=C/C–C, CN, CO, and CO bonds, respectively. Moreover, plasma surface treatment generated abundant surficial oxygen-containing functional groups; mainly CO, O–C=O, and CO bonds at 531.6, 532.9, and 534.4 eV, respectively. The N content in the material was mainly due to PPy and led to three nitrogen binding states at 401.2, 400.5, and 398.5 eV corresponding to graphite N, pyrrole N, and pyridine N structures, respectively. Only limited amounts of N and Ag were detected in the reference Ag/GDs/CFs. The N 1s peak indicated a small amount of pyridine N because the nitrogen in Ag/GDs/CFs was mainly derived from the CFs, whose pyridine content decreased at high temperatures. The limited Ag content was correlated with the relatively small CF specific surface area, which limited Ag-NP loading (Fig. S3).

Table S1 lists the elemental compositions of the CF-based series of electrodes. The original CF surface was mainly composed of C, N, and O, with 87.11 % C content. After plasma treatment and carbonization of the PPy film, the O and N contents of the Ag/NC/CF composites increased significantly. Owing to the large specific surface area of the PPy film, the Ag content was 0.35 %. Although the GDs provided sufficient active sites for Ag nucleation in the Ag/GD/CF composites, the space provided for the nucleated Ag was relatively limited because of the relatively low specific surface area of the CFs, leading to a low Ag content. Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs combined the advantages of the first two materials using the nitrogen-rich structure formed after the carbonization of the PPy film, which provided wettability to the surface of the material and sufficient space for Ag loading. Further loading of the GDs led to the nucleation and aggregation of Ag to form flower clusters, which increased the Ag content, as confirmed by the SEM images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviors of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S4a–e corresponds to 0.8, 1.0, 1.1, 1.2, and 1.3 g of pyrrole monomer, respectively. With an increase in the pyrrole monomer mass, the PPy film gradually became irregular with an uneven spherical spore shape. When the monomer content was high, the spore balls on the surface of the material were stacked into rod-like structures. After carbonization, the PPy film exhibited a certain degree of shedding. The polymer film on the surface of the PPy/CFs with a low pyrrole monomer quality decomposed and fell off at high temperatures, exposing the internal CFs groove-like structure. Despite the shedding of the PPy film, the original irregular morphology of the PPy/CFs was maintained when the quality of the pyrrole monomer was high. It also provided high specific surface areas when converted into nitrogen-rich carbon nanomaterials. The DPV curve shows that the material exhibits the best electrochemical performance at a pyrrole monomer mass of 1.2 g (Fig. S5). Simultaneously, the deposition voltage and time were optimized to 4 V and 120 s, respectively (Figs. S6–S9). In the carbonization process, the temperature and heating rate are important factors that affect the results. Fig. S10 shows that the material exhibits the best electrochemical performance at a carbonization temperature of 475 °C and heating rate of 0.5 °C min−1.

The differences in the electrochemical performances of the different CF-based modified electrodes were investigated using CV. Fig. 4 shows the CV curves of CFs, NCs/CFs, GDs/NCs/CFs, and Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.2) in the presence of HQ and CC. HQ and CC did not exhibit a current response on the CF surface before modification. Conversely, after surface modification with PPy-derived carbon, GDs, and Ag-NPs, the electrodes showed electrocatalytic activity toward HQ and CC. Electrocatalytic performance was attributed to the electrical conductivity of the GDs and Ag-NPs and their synergistic effects. In addition, owing to the rough PPy surface and high nitrogen content, the concurrent hydrogen bond formation between N atoms and HQ and CC led to electron transfer between the target and electrode surface, resulting in a higher current peak. Through the confinement effect of the GDs, sufficient active sites were provided for Ag-NP nucleation, which further improved the electroactive surface area of the material. Electrical conductivity increased the current response to HQ and CC. The peak spacing between the two anode peaks was 115 mV, indicating the absence of interference between them, with a redox peak potential difference (ΔE p) of 93 mV. Consequently, Ag/GD/NC/CF composites can be employed to prepare sensitive electrochemical sensors for the simultaneous detection of HQ and CC. Fig. S11 shows the electrochemical performance of Ag/NCs/CFs and Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs on HQ and CC detection. When Ag-NPs are converted into clusters under the confined effect of GDs, the current response are significantly improved, indicating that the cluster structure can promote the electrocatalytic ability.

In addition, CV (Fig. S12a) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. S12b) tests were performed in K3[Fe(CN)6]3−/4- to investigate the electrochemical behavior of NCs/CFs, GDs/NCs/CFs, Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs. The effective electroactive surface areas of the electrodes were estimated using the Randles–Sevcik equation (Eq. (1)) [16].
(1)
I p
= 2.69 ×
10 5
A
D
1 / 2
n
3 / 2
v
1 / 2
C ,
where I p is the redox peak-to-peak current, n is the number of transferred electrons (n = 1 in K3[Fe(CN)6]3−/4- redox systems), A is the effective surface area, D is the diffusion coefficient of the probe molecule (6.67 × 10−6 cm−2 s−1), C is the solution concentration (5 mM), and υ is the scan rate. The calculated electroactive surface areas of NCs/CFs, GDs/NCs/CFs, and Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs were 0.2037, 0.2139, and 0.2433 cm2, respectively. The charge transfer resistances (Rct) of the different electrodes were determined using a Nyquist diagram, and the Rct values of NCs/CFs, GDs/NCs/CFs, and Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs were 17.83, 21.70, and 27.91 Ω respectively. The charge transfer rate (ks) of the three electrodes can be calculated using Eq. (2) [16].
(2)
Rct = RT /
n 2
F 2
Cks ,
where R is the gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), T is the temperature (298 K), and F is the Faraday constant (96,485C mol−1). The ks values of NCs/CFs, GDs/NCs/CFs, and Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs were 0.19, 0.25, and 0.30 cm s−1 respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of HQ and CC
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S13 shows the effect of pH on the detection of 0.2 mM HQ and CC for Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs in PBS (0.1 M.) The peak currents of HQ and CC first increased and then decreased in a pH range of 6.2–8.2; the maximum value was obtained at pH 7.2. At low pH values, the protonated surface of the Ag/GD/NC/CF composites affected Π-Π interaction and hydrogen bonding between the material surface and the targets. Therefore, adsorption sites were blocked by phosphates and the adsorption capacity of the Ag/GD/NC/CF composites for the targets was reduced. At higher pH values, the negative charge on the surface of the Ag/GD/NC/CF composites increased, and some targets were in anionic form. The electrostatic repulsion between the electrode and analyte inhibited the adsorption of HQ and CC, which in turn resulted in a low peak current. Therefore, a pH of 7.2 was chosen as the optimal pH value for the reaction.

The effects of the scan rate on the responses of HQ and CC were explored using CV, and the kinetic processes on the surface of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs were evaluated. Higher scan rates gradually increased the redox peak currents of HQ and CC. Their oxidation peak potentials shifted positively and their reduction peak potentials shifted negatively, indicating the quasi-reversibility of the electrochemical responses of the three targets on the Ag/GD/NC/CF electrodes (Fig. 5a). Moreover, the peak currents of HQ and CC varied linearly with the square root of the scan rate (Fig. 6b and c). The linear equations Ipa = 0.7955v1/2–2.9139 and Ipa = 0.8878v1/2–2.5593, indicate that the HQ and CC processes on the surface of the Ag/GD/NC/CF electrodes were diffusion-controlled.

To explore the detection of HQ and CC by the Ag/GD/NC/CF electrode under optimal conditions, the analytical performance of the electrochemical sensor was studied using the amperometric i–t response because of its high sensitivity and resolution. Fig. 7a and b shows the current changes of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs to detect HQ and CC in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.2) at concentrations ranging from 1 to 800 μM. As the concentration of the target substrate increased, the current increased in a stepwise manner. The results showed a good linear relationship. The linear equations for HQ and CC are expressed in Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively.
(3)
I p
= 0.1195 c + 2.0951 ,
(4)
I p
= 0.1822 c + 4.5039 .

The slopes of the linear equations for Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs indicated high sensitivity (HQ 0.1195 μA μM−1 cm−2, CC 0.1822 μA μM−1 cm−2), with detection limits of 0.045 and 0.028 μM for HQ and CC, respectively. Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs showed good detection of HQ and CC, mainly because the PPy-derived carbon nanofilms provided abundant specific surface area, which promoted the electron transfer rate between the electrode surface and analyte and significantly enhanced the oxidation current to the target. The XPS results revealed high pyridine and pyrrolic nitrogen content in the material that formed hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl group of the target, which was beneficial for electron transfer. In particular, the flower-cluster-like Ag-NPs formed during the confinement of the GDs greatly enhanced their electrocatalytic ability. The assembly of individual Ag-NPs into Ag clusters not only achieves the maximum utilization of each atom but also provides continuous active sites for catalytic processes that require multiple active sites, thus greatly improving the electrochemical performance. Table 1 presents a performance comparison of the fabricated sensor with other sensors possessing relatively high sensitivities and wide linear ranges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of Pb2+ and Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to HQ and CC, the detection performance of Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs for Pb2+ and Cd2+ was discussed, and the detection conditions for Pb2+ and Cd2+ were optimized. First, the enrichment time and potential of Pb2+ were adjusted (Fig. S14a and b). When the enrichment time was 420 s and the enrichment potential was −1.0 V, the highest peak current was obtained. Therefore, these values were selected as the subsequent experimental conditions. In addition, current variation was investigated under different pH conditions, and the optimal effect was observed at pH 3.5 (Fig. S14c). When the pH was low, the H ions produced by ionization in the solution competed with Pb2+ and Cd2+ for binding sites, thus weakening the signal. With increasing pH, the concentration of hydroxide ions in the solution increased, and metal hydroxides were easily formed with Pb2+ and Cd2+, which led to a corresponding weakening of the dissolution peak signal.

Under optimal conditions, the detection performance of the Ag/GDs/NCs/CFs for Cd2+ and Pb2+ was studied using DPV. In the concentration range of 12.5–250 μM, the current signal is linearly enhanced with increasing substrate concentration (Fig. 7a and b). The linear equations for Cd2+ and Pb2+ are expressed in Eqs. (5) and (6), respectively:
(5)
I p
= 0.2169 c + 0.0808 ,
(6)
I p
= 0.2017 c + 0.1651 .

Cd2+ and Pb2+ sensitivity was 0.2169 and 0.2017 μA μM−1 cm−2, respectively, and their detection limits reached 24.8 and 25.2 nM respectively. Notably, the Ag/GD/NC/CF electrode is a low-cost, environmentally friendly, and versatile electrode material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference, stability, and reproducibility of the electrode
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of the Ag/GD/NC/CF electrode materials, the selection of interfering substances was tested. Ten times the concentrations of Dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), glucose (Glu), and p-nitrophenol (4-NP) were added as HQ and CC disruptors, respectively (Fig. S15a). As Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, and Hg2+ interfere with Cd2+ and Pb2+ (Fig. S15b), the Ag/GD/NC/CF electrodes exhibit excellent anti-interference performance. The reproducibility and stability of the Ag/GD/NC/CF electrodes were tested using six identical electrodes (Fig. S15c) and the same electrode at 3-d intervals for 15 d (Fig. S15d), respectively. However, after 15 d, the detection effect of HQ and CC reached 86.2 % and 85.6 % of the initial state, respectively, which can be attributed to the easy oxidation of the Ag-NP phase compared to that of other noble metal nanoparticles.

To study the practicality of the sensor, water samples were collected from the Burhatton River in Yanji City. No HQ, CC, Cd2+, or Pb2+ signals were found in the water samples; therefore, the marking method was selected for determination. The pH for HQ and CC detection was adjusted to 7.2, whereas that for Cd2+ and Pb2+ detection was adjusted to 3.5. The recoveries of HQ, CC, Cd2+, and Pb2+ were 98.64–101.18 % (Table 2 ). These results indicate that the electrochemical sensor constructed in this study is promising for the detection of HQ, CC, Cd2+, and Pb2+ in real water samples.

One-dimensional PPy-derived carbon nanofilms and zero-dimensional GDs films were prepared on CF surfaces using simple electrochemical polymerization and electrodeposition methods to improve the morphology and distribution of Ag-NPs. The prepared electrodes enabled the detection of HQ, CC, Cd2+, and Pb2+. These excellent properties were due to the surface modification of the flower cluster of the Ag-NPs and GDs double nanozymes and the abundant pyridine nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen, which promoted electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic activity. This study presents a straightforward and effective strategy for fabricating CF-based flexible electrochemical sensors for detecting phenolic pollutants and heavy metal ions in freshwater watersheds. The prepared sensor can be used for the determination of phenolic pollutants and heavy metal ions in real samples.
Zhi-Yuan Feng: Writing – original draft. Jin-Chi Jiang: Data curation. Long-Yue Meng: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22166034, 51703192); the Project of Department of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (China) (YDZJ202201ZYTS542); the Innovation Team Project of Yanbian University (China) and the Higher Education Discipline Innovation Project (111 Project, D18012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111074.
